segments.s381-g81: سر آن را همه خواند is a past-tense clause: خواند (he read/recited) takes the definite object marked by را. آن refers back to the thing being announced; سر here means 'the matter/summary' (an older, somewhat literary use). همه after the object emphasizes that it was done for everyone. گفتا is an archaic past form of گفت (’he said’), followed immediately by the imperative روید as reported speech.
segments.s381-g82: This is the direct speech content of what he said. سپاه پراگنده is a noun phrase 'the scattered army/troops'; گرد آورید is an imperative (plural) made of گرد + آوردید, where گرد‌آوردن means 'to gather/collect' and آورید functions as the imperative 'bring/gather'. The command thus orders: gather the scattered troops.
segments.s381-g83: برفتند is the simple past, third-person plural 'they departed/they went off.' گردان لشکر is a noun group where گردان (a troop/division) qualifies لشکر (army) — 'regiments/divisions of the army.' همه after the phrase emphasizes totality ('all the regiments'). Word order reflects older/high literary Persian where modifiers and quantifiers can appear after the head.
segments.s381-g84: به introduces destination and governs a series of places: کوه و بیابان و جای رمه — 'to the mountain and the wilderness and the place of the herds' (i.e., pastures). جای رمه is a genitive-like compound 'place of the flock/herds.' The coordinates are connected by و ('and').
segments.s381-g85: بدو is the prepositional/old-form 'to him' (i.e., 'toward him' / 'to his side'); باز means 'back' or 'again.' خواندند is past plural 'they called/summoned.' لشکرش را is the definite object 'his army' marked by را. Together: 'they called his army back to him.'
segments.s381-g86: This continues the list of those summoned: گزیده سواران is a noun phrase 'the chosen/elite horsemen.' کشورش را is another object phrase 'of his country/realm' with را marking it as a definite object; the structure implies they called back both his army and his chosen riders of the realm.
segments.s381-g57: به + place (به زاول) marks direction/place: here 'به' (archaic/poetic word-order) introduces the location 'Zāvol'. نشستست is an older, poetic 3rd-person singular past form ('he sat' / 'he settled') with the enclitic -ست; با + لشکرش means 'with his army' (با = 'with', لشکرش = 'his army', ش = possessive). The whole chunk states who sat where and with whom.
segments.s381-g58: This clause has an inverted, emphatic word order common in epic poetry: سواری نه 'no horseman' (negation particle نه follows the noun for emphasis), اندر همه کشورش = 'in/throughout all his country' (اندر = 'in/within', همه کشورش = 'all his realm'). Together it says there were no riders/cavalry anywhere in his land.
segments.s381-g59: کنونست is an emphatic, poetic copular phrase meaning 'it is now' or 'now it is'. هنگام 'time/moment' combines with the following infinitive phrase کین خواستن 'to seek vengeance' (کین = 'vengeance', خواستن here = 'to seek/want'), so the whole segment states that the present moment is that for seeking revenge.
segments.s381-g60: بباید is the modal 'it is necessary / one must' (poetic form of باید). It governs the following verbs: بسیچید and آراستن are infinitival/verbal forms meaning 'to muster/gather (in force)' and 'to array/equip/prepare'. The coordinating و links the two actions: one must both gather forces and equip/array them.
segments.s381-g61: پسرش = 'his son' (پسر + possessive ش). آن here is a demonstrative/identifying article ('that/the') used poetically before a title; گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'precious/esteemed' modifying the name اسفندیار (Esfandiyar). The phrase identifies the person.
segments.s381-g62: به بند = 'in/into bonds' (به marking state or location), گران‌اندرست is a compound adjective meaning 'heavy/weighty' or 'grave' describing the bonds, and استوار is an adjective meaning 'firm/steady' or 'securely fixed'. Together they describe Esfandiyar as being securely fastened in heavy fetters.
segments.s381-g63: کدامست is the poetic interrogative 'which is it?' functioning like 'who is there?'. مردی is an indefinite 'a man', and پژوهنده راز is a descriptive noun phrase 'a seeker/inquirer of secrets' (پژوهنده = 'inquirer/seeker', راز = 'secret'). The segment asks for the identity of such a man.
segments.s381-g64: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that', and پیماید is the poetic 3rd-person form meaning 'traverses / will traverse / can accomplish' — here used as 'will/ can traverse'. این ژرف راه دراز = 'this deep, long road' (این = 'this', ژرف = 'deep/profound', راه = 'road/path', دراز = 'long'). The clause asks who will undertake/accomplish passage of this difficult route.
segments.s381-g25: بگشتند is a past plural verb ('they turned/turned away'); here it begins the clause (poetic V‑initial order) and is followed by the adverb یکسر ('entirely/wholly') and a prepositional phrase ز فرمان شاه ('from the king's command'). ز is the poetic variant of modern از meaning 'from'. The whole segment means 'they completely turned away from the king's order.'
segments.s381-g26: بهم is a reciprocal/collective marker ('to one another' / 'together') showing the action is mutual; برشکستند is past plural 'they broke' (here 'broke/violated'); پیمان شاه is the object 'the king's covenant/pledge'. The lack of an object marker (را) is typical in poetry; the clause means 'they mutually broke the king's covenant.'
segments.s381-g27: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. آگاهی آمد is a light verb construction 'news/word came' and به بهمن marks the recipient ('to Bahman'). که begins the reported content ('that ...'). The word order (verb before the indirect object) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s381-g28: ببستست is an archaic past form of بستن meaning 'he had bound/fastened' (third person). آن شیر را is the definite object marked by را ('that/the lion' as object) and بی‌گناه postmodifies شیر meaning 'innocent'. Together: 'had bound that innocent lion.'
segments.s381-g29: نبرده functions here as a participial adjective formed from بردن with the negative prefix ن-, placed before گزینان to qualify them: 'not taken/captured.' گزینان اسفندیار means 'Esfandiyar's chosen/select men' (genitive relationship). In short: 'Esfandiyar's chosen men who had not been taken.'
segments.s381-g30: ازانجا برفتند is a simple past clause 'from there they departed' (برفتند = 'they went/left'). تیماردار follows as a noun in apposition/title ('the attendant/steward') associated with the departing group; the plural verb agrees with the plural 'گزینان' mentioned earlier. The phrase thus indicates the chosen men left that place, with the attendant/steward (tīmārdār).
segments.s381-g31: همی داشتند is a progressive/habitual construction ('they were ... / they kept ...'); داشتند here functions as an auxiliary meaning 'were holding/keeping.' از سپه دست باز is an idiomatic phrase: از سپه 'from the army/host' and دست باز ('hand free') meaning to withdraw one's hand/power or to withdraw support/control. Together: 'they were withdrawing their hold/support from the host.'
segments.s381-g32: پس اندر گرفتند is a temporal sequence 'then/afterwards they took/entered' (گرفتند = 'they took/seized'); راه دراز is the object 'the long road/path.' اندر is the archaic preposition 'into/in' used in poetic diction. The clause means 'then they took the long way.'
segments.s381-g9: This clause names a person and gives a relative clause: 'ابا پیر دستان' is a name/title (Aba Pir-Dastan). كِه introduces the relative clause 'که بودش پدر' = 'who was his father'. بودش is the past of بودن with the enclitic -ش marking 'his/its', so the structure is 'who was his father.' Word order is name + relative clause; poetic ellipsis allows omission of an overt relative pronoun in translation.
segments.s381-g10: This short clause states membership or rank: 'ابا مهتران و گزینان در'. Here مهتران ('the great/elders/chiefs') and گزینان ('the chosen/select') form a coordinated nominal group with در ('among/in') functioning postpositionally; the initial ابا repeats the name/title as topic. The line uses poetic word order (topic first, predicate reduced).
segments.s381-g11: به شادی ('with joy') is an adverbial phrase (preposition + noun). پذیره is a nominal/adjectival stem meaning 'receptive/received' and combines with the past plural شد(ند) plus enclitic ش (object 'him') — پذیرا شدند + ش → 'they received him'. به راه ('on the road') is a following locative adverbial. Note the auxiliary-like use of شدن with پذیره to mark the event.
segments.s381-g12: ازو ('because of him / from him') is an ablative/causal prepositional-like clitic. شادمان گشت = 'became joyful' (شادمان adjective + گشت 'became'). فرخنده شاه places the epithet before the noun ('the fortunate king'); in Persian epics an epithet may precede the title. Overall: 'The fortunate king became glad because of him.'
segments.s381-g13: به زاولش is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from the outset / at once' (به + زاول + enclitic ش). بردند is the past plural 'they took/led (in)'. مهمان خویش is the object 'their guest' (مهمان + خوی/خویش indicating 'own/closely related' with the enclitic ش marking possession). The clause is a compact narrative perfect for epic style.
segments.s381-g14: همه ('all/everyone') is the subject; بنده‌وار is an adverbial/locutional adjective meaning 'servilely / like servants' modifying ایستادند ('they stood'). پیش functions as a prepositional/adverbial particle 'before / in the presence of' (object omitted but understood). The verb is past plural, so 'All stood before him humbly.'
segments.s381-g15: وزو (an older form for وضو/وزّو) here functions as a noun 'ablution/washing' marking a ritual act; the following element and construction indicate a sequence of actions. کشتی بیاموختند = 'they learned wrestling' (کشتی 'wrestling' + بیاموختند past plural 'they learned'). The internal connective (and/or the prepositional element 'from him') is compressed poetically; the clause lists acts performed with the guest as teacher/performer.
segments.s381-g16: This clause continues the series of actions: ببستند is past plural 'they bound/tied/they closed' (contextual reading depends on what is being bound), و is 'and', آذر 'fire', برافروختند 'they kindled/they lit up'. The verb brafrokhtan is a compound verb (بر + افروختن) in past plural, here meaning 'kindled'.
segments.s381-g1: برآمد is a past narrative verb 'arose/appeared'. بسی here is an intensive adverb meaning 'much/very' (poetic usage). روزگاری is a noun 'time/age' and بدوی is an adjective 'ancient/primeval' modifying روزگاری; the whole phrase is a temporal frame 'a very ancient time arose' (i.e. 'in very ancient times'). Word order is poetic (verb before modifiers).
segments.s381-g2: که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that'). خسرو is the personal name (here the king). سوی + place is the prepositional expression 'toward Sīstān' (سوی = toward, سیستان = the region). کرد + روی is a light-verb construction: کرد 'made/did' combines with روی 'face' to mean 'turned his face (toward)' or more generally 'set out/took direction toward'. The verb کرد is past tense, so the clause reports a completed action.
segments.s381-g3: که here links this purposive/explanatory clause ('so that/that'). آنجا is the locative adverb 'there'. کند is the subjunctive/optative form of کردن used as an auxiliary 'may he do' or 'he would do' in narrative. زند is a verb-form that in this poetic context means 'place/establish' (the given dictionary senses don't match exactly, so it behaves as a lexical verb following کند). و connects the verbs. استا (from ایستا/ایستادن) means 'set/stand' or 'make stand', and روا is an adverb/adjective 'proper/fitting' qualifying the action. Together the verbs express establishing/setting something properly at that place.
segments.s381-g4: کند again is the auxiliary/lexical verb 'does/makes' (here marking the purposive action). موبدان is the plural of موبد 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest) and را is the Persian definite/object marker marking موبدان as the object. بدانجا is another locative phrase 'to that place/there'. گوا in this line functions as 'witness' (i.e. 'make them witnesses there'). The clause therefore says he caused the mobeds to be witnesses at that place.
segments.s381-g5: جو is an archaic/poetic conjunction (equivalent to چو 'when'); it does not mean 'barley' here (the supplied lexical senses don't reflect this usage). آنجا رسید is the past-tense verb phrase 'arrived there'. آن گرانمایه شاه is an appositive noun phrase 'that precious/noble king' serving as the subject; the word order is inverted for poetic effect (temporal clause first, subject after).
segments.s381-g6: پذیره is a noun 'reception/acceptance' and شدش is a past auxiliary with enclitic ش (sh) meaning 'to/for him' — together 'a reception was given to him' or 'he was received'. پهلوان سپاه is the agent phrase 'the champion (pehlavān) of the host/army' (پهلوان = champion/hero, سپاه = army/host). The line literally: 'a reception was made for him by the champion of the army' — i.e. the champion received/welcomed him.
segments.s381-g7: شه نیمروز = 'the shah (king) of Nimrūz' (نیمروز here is the regional name Nimrūz, not 'noon'). آنک is an archaic relative 'who/which'. رستمش is the name 'Rostam' with the enclitic ش (here forming the predicate 'whose name was Rostam' when followed by نام). نام is the predicate noun 'name'. The construction is a poetic relative/appositive: 'the Shah of Nimrūz, who was named Rostam'.
segments.s381-g8: سوار is a descriptive noun 'horseman/rider' (here used as an epithet). جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly-wise' or 'experienced in the world', modifying سوار. همتای is a compound of همتا + ی, 'equal of / match for', and سام is the proper name 'Sām' — so the phrase says he is a horseman well-traveled/experienced and equal to Sām. The modifiers follow typical epic word order where epithets cluster after the noun.
segments.s381-g49: جز is an exception particle meaning “except,” placed before the pronoun ایشان (“them/they”); the locative phrase به بلخ اندرون (“in the inner part of Balkh”) follows, and the copula/negative نیست precedes کس in the inverted poetic order (نیست کس = there is no one). The overall word order is poetic: exception → place → negative verb → indefinite noun.
segments.s381-g50: از introduces a subset “from/among”; آهنگ‌داران is the plural noun “musicians.” همینند is a demonstrative + copula (“they are these” / “these are the ones”), and بس is a postpositive adverb meaning “enough” or “that suffices.” The phrase therefore narrows to “from the musicians, these are enough.”
segments.s381-g51: مگر here marks an exception (“except for”); it governs the following noun phrase پاسبانان کاخ همای, where پاسبانان is the plural “guards” and کاخ همای is a genitival noun phrase “the palace of Homay” (name/title). The whole segment therefore reads “except the guards of Homay’s palace.”
segments.s381-g52: هلا is an interjection (here an urging “come on!/holla!”); زود برخیز is an imperative phrase “rise quickly” (زود = quickly/soon + برخیز = stand up/arise). و coordinates the next unit; چندین is the quantifier “several.” مپای is an archaic/rare word whose exact meaning is uncertain in this passage (it appears here without clear modern gloss), so the clause should be read as an exhortation composed of interjection + imperative + coordinated phrase ending in an obscure noun.
segments.s381-g53: مهان را is the direct object with the object marker را (“the guests”); همه modifies مهان (“all the guests”). خواند here means “called/summoned” (past tense) with شاه as the subject—poetic word order places the object before verb and then the subject. چگل is a noun (“tattle/gossip” / “chatter”) appended after the clause, functioning parenthetically or as a qualifying note (a terse poetic tag).
segments.s381-g54: ابر جنگ is a compound epithet “cloud of war” (ابر = cloud, جنگ = war). لهراسپشان is the name لهراسپ with the possessive/third‑person plural suffix -شان, here indicating association with Lohrasp (roughly ‘Lohrasp’s’/‘to Lohrasp’s [men]’). داد is the past tense “gave,” and دل is the object “heart” used figuratively as “courage.” The clause therefore means that the war‑omen (or war-cloud) lent courage to Lohrasp’s side.
segments.s381-g55: بدانید is an imperative/formulaic verb “know” or “be informed” used to introduce reported speech; گفتا is the past tense “he said,” and که is the complementizer “that” introducing the clause reported. گشتاسپ شاه is the proper name plus title “King Gushtasp” and serves as the subject of the reported clause. The sequence is a conventional poetic introduction: “He said, ‘Know that King Gushtasp…’ ”
segments.s381-g56: سوی is the preposition “toward/for”; نیمروز here names the region or direction (Nimruz). او is a third‑person pronoun (“him/he”) and can function as the indirect object or the person left behind; سپردست is an adjective/noun/verb compound meaning to entrust or a custodian ('entrusted/one in charge') and راه is the direct object “road/route.” In context the phrase says that (the king) set out toward Nimruz and entrusted the road (custody of the route) to him—the construction is somewhat compressed and poetic but conveys ‘he left him as custodian of the road/route.’
segments.s381-g17: 'برآمد' is a past-tense/perfect verb meaning 'arose' or 'was held' and heads the clause. 'برین' (here written as the pieces 'بر' + 'ین') is a poetic adjective 'noble / high' modifying 'میهمانی' ('banquet'). 'دو سال' is a duration adverbial placed after the noun: literally 'two years' — so the whole segment means a great banquet came into being/was held lasting two years.
segments.s381-g18: The prefixed particle 'همی' gives a durative/continuous sense (classical Persian 'kept on/was continually'). 'خورد' is the past form of 'خوردن' here 'ate/kept eating'. The following nominal phrase 'گشتاسپ با پور زال' is the subject/agent: 'Gushtasp with the son of Zal.' Together: 'Gushtasp and Zal's son kept eating.'
segments.s381-g19: 'به هرجا کجا' is a repetitive/poetic locative expression meaning 'to every place / wherever'; it emphasizes universality. 'شهریاران' (plural) is the noun 'kings/lords' and 'بدند' is an older/polite plural past form meaning 'they were' (cf. بَودند). The whole clause: 'wherever kings were.'
segments.s381-g20: 'ازان کار' is a genitive/relational phrase 'of that affair / from that deed'. The verb phrase 'آگه شدند' is past tense 'became aware / learned' with شدند as the past plural auxiliary; 'گشتاسپ' is the (grammatical) subject. Word order places the prepositional phrase first, then subject and verb: 'Gushtasp became aware of that matter.'
segments.s381-g21: The relative marker 'که' introduces the clause. Word order is Subject ('او' = he) — indirect/object clitic ('مر' = me / to me) — direct object ('سر پهلوان را' = 'the champion's head' with the object marker 'را') — verb ('ببست' = bound/tied). In older Persian 'مر' is the clitic for 'me / to me'; the verb 'ببست' is a past tense of 'بستن' here meaning 'bound/tied'.
segments.s381-g22: The noun 'تن' (body) is followed by a descriptive compound 'پیل وارش' (= 'elephant‑like / like an elephant') — here poetic composition where 'پیل' supplies the simile. 'به آهن' is a prepositional phrase 'in/with iron' and 'بخست' is a past verb form of 'بستن' used here in the sense 'closed up / clothed / fastened' (poetic usage meaning 'was clad/armed with'). The clause says his elephantine body was (or he clad it) in iron.
segments.s381-g23: The first 'به' marks direction/destination 'to Zavolistan' and 'شد' is the past tense 'became'. The second 'به' before 'پیغمبری' is predicative: 'became to prophethood' = 'became a prophet.' So the phrase reads 'He went to Zavolistan and became a prophet.'
segments.s381-g24: The relative 'که' introduces a subordinate clause describing that prophetic role. 'نفرین کند' uses the present/subjunctive form 'کند' after the noun 'نفرین' to mean 'who will/have the function of cursing' (classical Persian uses present subjunctive for reported/general actions). 'بر بت آزری' is 'upon the idol of Azar' (or 'Azari's idol'). So: 'who would curse the idol of Azar.'
segments.s381-g41: خود is an emphatic subject pronoun placed before the rest of the clause ('he himself'). از + place names marks the origin ('from Balkh'); the classical preposition زی introduces the destination ('to Zabulistan'). کشید is a poetic past tense (third person) of کشیدن used here intransitively to mean 'moved/went' or 'set out.' Word order is Subject — origin — destination — verb (emphatic subject first).
segments.s381-g42: بیابان گذارید is a compact phrase meaning 'he crossed/passed the desert'; گذارید here is a poetic/past verbal form conveying passage (see note). The conjunction و links that clause to سیحون بدید, where بدید is the older past form of دیدن meaning 'he saw' and سیحون is the river-name object. The sequence is verb phrase (crossed the desert) then 'and' + verb phrase (saw the Sihon).
segments.s381-g43: به زاول is an adverbial phrase: به here gives a locative/temporal sense ('at' or 'in') and زاٌول (classical/poetic usage) means 'at the outset' or 'at first'. نشستست is an archaic past form meaning 'sat' (third person). Important word order: temporal/adverbial phrase first, then verb, then the noun phrase 'مهمان زال' ('Zal as guest'), where the noun مهمان ('guest') is followed by the name زال.
segments.s381-g44: برین روزگاران is a time phrase: بر + ین (an older/poetic form roughly 'in these') + روزگاران ('days/times') = 'in those days/at that time'. برآمد is the simple past 'arose/happened/passed' used here to mean that a period elapsed, and دو سال is the duration 'two years.' The phrase order is temporal phrase → verb → duration.
segments.s381-g45: به بلخ اندرونست places the locative first: به بلخ = 'in/at Balkh' and اندرونست is a compound/poetic copula meaning 'is inside' (present/state). لهراسپ شاه is the apposition 'Lohrasp, the king' (name + title). The clause therefore reads 'Lohrasp the king was inside Balkh.'
segments.s381-g46: نماندست is the negative past form meaning 'did not remain' or 'there remained none.' از introduces the group from which nothing remains ('of the Iranians'), ایرانیان is the plural noun 'Iranians', و links the second coordinated noun سپاه ('army/host'). The whole clause is a negative existential: 'there remained none of the Iranians and the army.'
segments.s381-g47: مگر is an exclusive particle meaning 'except' or 'only.' It introduces the exception: هفتصد (seven hundred) modifies مرد ('men, warriors'), and آتش‌پرست is a compound noun 'fire‑worshipper' describing those men. Word order here is 'except [number + men + epithet]'.
segments.s381-g48: This small clause describes those men’s posture and action: هه is an interjection/exclamation (a shout, here rendered 'Ha!' or 'Heh!'), پیش آذر is a fixed classical prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of the fire' (آذر = fire), برآورده is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'raised' or 'lifted,' and دست ('hand') is the noun 'hands.' The order 'برآورده دست' = 'hands raised' (participial adjective after the noun phrase) and together the phrase means they stood before the fire with hands raised while shouting.
segments.s381-g33: به + NP marks movement toward a person/place (here ‘to’). پیش‌گو is a title/noun placed before the proper name (so: ‘the soothsayer Esfandiyar’). آمدند is the past plural of آمدن (“they came”); the verb appears at the end as customary in Persian.
segments.s381-g34: کیان‌زادگان is the subject noun ‘royal offspring/princes’. شیروار is an adverbial modifier meaning ‘all together/at once’ that follows the noun and precedes the verb. آمدند is the past plural verb ‘they came’ agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s381-g35: پدر را uses را to mark a definite/direct object (‘the father’). به رامش is a prepositional phrase indicating the state (‘in/into peace’); همی + داشتند is an older/perfective-progressive pattern where همی gives ongoing/continuous nuance and داشتند functions as the past auxiliary/verb (‘they kept / they maintained’).
segments.s381-g36: به + زندانش indicates direction/place ‘to/into his prison’ (زندانش = زندان + -ش ‘his’). تنها is an adverb ‘alone/only’. بنگذاشتند is a past plural verb meaning here ‘they left/placed (him)’, with the verb at the clause end as usual.
segments.s381-g37: پس here means ‘then/afterwards’. آگاهی آمد is a common idiom ‘news/information came’ (i.e. ‘news arrived’); به سالار چین marks the recipient of the news (‘to the commander of China’), with the head noun سالار followed by the place-name چین.
segments.s381-g38: که introduces a subordinate clause ‘that …’. شاه is the subject. از + گمان indicates cause or source (‘from/out of suspicion’). اندرآمد is used here as a past-form verb ‘entered into / fell into’ (literary use) and is followed by به کین ‘into hatred/vengeance’—together meaning the king came to feel/was seized by hatred.
segments.s381-g39: برآشفت is a past intransitive verb ‘became angry/was enraged’ (literary). خسرو is the subject (the king’s name). به + اسفندیار marks the target/recipient of that anger ‘with/against Esfandiyar’ (literal ‘to Esfandiyar’ showing who the anger is directed at).
segments.s381-g40: به زندان و بندش combines two objects of destination/condition: به زندان ‘to prison’ and بندش (‘his bindings’ / ‘bonds’) linked by و. فرستاد is the transitive past verb ‘he sent’ (here: ‘he had him sent’). خوار is a predicative/adjunct meaning ‘humiliated/despised’ describing the manner or result of the sending.
segments.s381-g73: پژوهنده راز is a noun phrase 'the seeker (of) the secret' with پژوهنده (active participle/noun) modified by the genitive راز. پیمود is a past tense verb 'traversed/took' and راه is its object 'the road/path'; word order is poetic (verb before object).
segments.s381-g74: به بلخ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into Balkh'. گزین شد is a compound in which گزین 'choose' appears with شد; here شد functions like a narrative/auxiliary ('it came about' / 'it was decided/chosen'), and که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/where') giving the circumstance 'بد گاه شاه' = 'at an unlucky time the king...'.
segments.s381-g75: ندید here functions as the past tense 'he saw' (classical orthography), followed by the locative اندرون شاه 'in the king's inner [chamber]'. گشتاسپ را is the direct object (را marks definiteness). The clause order is verb + locative + object, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s381-g76: پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a worshipper' (the -ای suffix). دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' again; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; لهراسپ را is another definite direct object 'Lohrasp' marked by را. The clause lists multiple things seen.
segments.s381-g77: بشد is a narrative past 'it happened/so it came about', همچنان 'thus/in the same way'. پیش خاقان is a prepositional phrase 'before the khaghan (in his presence)'. بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (ب- prefix is an archaic/perfective marker).
segments.s381-g78: به رخ پیش او is an adverbial phrase 'before his face / in front of him' (به رخ = 'to the face', پیش او = 'before him'). زمین را is the definite object 'the ground' (را marks it). برفت is the main verb meaning here 'fell' or 'went down' (archaic/poetic past); note the supplied lemma senses list does not match this usage, so the exact dictionary sense is not available in the provided options.
segments.s381-g79: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. ارجاسپ is the subject. آگاه شد is a verbal phrase 'became aware' (آگاه adjective + شد auxiliary 'became'). شاد گشت is another predication 'he became glad' (شاد + گشت 'became'), a common doubled-copula pattern in classical Persian.
segments.s381-g80: از اندوه دیرینه is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of long-standing sorrow' (دیرینه modifies اندوه). آزاد گشت is the past 'became free' (آزاد adjective + گشت 'became'), describing liberation from grief.
segments.s381-g65: نراند is a third-person past-form with the negative prefix ن- (i.e., 'did not drive' / 'does not drive'); به راه is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the road'; ایچ is an archaic-word used as a noun/adjective meaning 'nothing/naught' modifying راه ('the path of nothingness'); و links the two coordinated phrases; بی‌ره is a compound modifier meaning 'without-road' or 'roadless' that modifies the following word; رود here is the verb 'goes' in a classical/poetic form (so the phrase means 'and (he/they) go on a roadless course').
segments.s381-g66: ز ایران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز used in poetic register); هراسان و آگه are two adjectives joined by و ('frightened and aware/knowing') describing the subject that follows; رود at the end is the verb 'goes' or 'depart(s)' in Ferdowsi's poetic style, so the whole segment means 'they depart from Iran, frightened and aware'.
segments.s381-g67: یکی is an indefinite particle 'a certain' (introducing someone); جادوی here functions as a substantive derived from 'magic' meaning 'a magician' or 'a magical being'; بود is the past copula 'was'; نامش is the noun 'name' plus the possessive clitic -ش 'his' (i.e., 'his name'); ستوه functions as the personal name (it also has the lexical meaning 'weary' but here is used as the name).
segments.s381-g68: گذارنده is an active participle 'one who places/sets' or agentive noun 'placer/causer' and here works as an epithet; راه is its object 'the road/path'; و joins the second epithet; نهفته is an adjective 'hidden' or 'secret'; پژوه is a substantive/adjective 'seeker, searcher' — together the phrase lists two epithets of the magician ('one who affects the road' and 'a hidden-seeker').
segments.s381-g69: منم is emphatic first person 'I am' / 'it is I' (here the speaker identifying himself); گفت is the past verb 'said' (Ferdowsi often uses گفت/گفتا for 'said'); آهسته is an adverb 'softly/quietly'; و links the following vocative; نامجوی is a vocative/adjective 'name-seeker' (addressing the interlocutor as one seeking a name or renown).
segments.s381-g70: چه is the interrogative 'what'; باید is the modal 'must/should' expressing necessity; ت (here written separately) is the 2nd-person enclitic (t) combined with را (object marker) which follows to form ترا 'you' (object); هرچ is 'whatever/anything' and the second باید + بگوی is the verbal phrase 'ought to be said' (بگوی is the subjunctive/present stem of گفتن 'to say') — the clause asks 'what must I tell you? whatever must be said (I will say)'.
segments.s381-g71: شه here is 'king'; چینش in classical/poetic compounds functions to indicate 'of China' (so together 'the king of China' — an idiomatic compound in earlier Persian poetry); گفتا is the past-tense 'said'; به ایران is the prepositional phrase 'to/into Iran' (به + place); خرام is an imperative or jussive verb 'stride/prance' (addressing someone to enter/advance with a proud gait).
segments.s381-g72: نگهبان آتش is a noun phrase 'the guardian/keeper of the fire' (نگهبان = guard, آتش = fire); ببین is the imperative 'see/look' (an instruction); تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' and is followed by کدام 'which', an interrogative pronoun whose complement continues in the next line — together: 'look at the fire-keeper so that you may learn which ...'.

sentences.s381-s41: He read that matter aloud to all and said, “Go—gather the scattered troops.”
sentences.s381-s42: They deployed all the regiments of the army and went to the mountains, the wilderness, and the pasture-lands.
sentences.s381-s43: They called back to him his army and the chosen horsemen of his realm.
sentences.s381-s29: He sat at Zāvol with his army; there was not a single horseman throughout his realm.
sentences.s381-s30: Now is the time to seek vengeance; one must muster (a great force) and make ready.
sentences.s381-s31: His son, the noble Esfandiyar, was firmly held in heavy bonds.
sentences.s381-s32: Which man, an inquirer into secrets, will traverse this deep, long road?
sentences.s381-s13: They wholly turned away from the king's command; they mutually broke the king's covenant.
sentences.s381-s14: When news reached Bahman that the king had bound that innocent lion.
sentences.s381-s15: Esfandiyar's chosen men, not taken, departed from there — with their attendant/steward.
sentences.s381-s16: They were withdrawing their support from the host; then they took the long road.
sentences.s381-s5: Aba Pir-Dastan, who was his father; Aba stood among the chiefs and the chosen.
sentences.s381-s6: They received him with joy on the road; the fortunate king became glad because of him.
sentences.s381-s7: At once they led their guest in; all stood before him like servants.
sentences.s381-s8: They made ablutions and learned wrestling from him; they tied (things) and kindled the fire.
sentences.s381-s1: In very ancient times there came a moment when Khosrow turned his face toward Sīstān.
sentences.s381-s2: so that there he might set things and firmly establish what was fitting, and make the mobeds witnesses in that place.
sentences.s381-s3: When that noble king arrived there, the champion of the host received him.
sentences.s381-s4: The Shah of Nimrūz, who was named Rustam — a worldly horseman, a match for Sām.
sentences.s381-s25: There is no one inside Balkh except them; as for the musicians, these suffice.
sentences.s381-s26: Except for the palace-guards of Homay. “Hala — rise quickly!” he cried, “and … (چندین مپای).”
sentences.s381-s27: The king summoned all the guests; the cloud of war gave courage to Lohrasp’s (men).
sentences.s381-s28: He said, “Know that King Gushtasp set out for Nimruz; he entrusted the road (custody of the route) to him.”
sentences.s381-s9: A splendid banquet was held; Gushtasp and Zal’s son kept eating for two years.
sentences.s381-s10: Wherever there were kings, Gushtasp became aware of that affair.
sentences.s381-s11: That he bound the champion's head to me; he clad his elephant‑like body in iron.
sentences.s381-s12: He went to Zavolistan and became a prophet, one who would curse Azari's idol.
sentences.s381-s21: He himself departed from Balkh for Zabulistan, crossed the desert and saw the Sihon.
sentences.s381-s22: At the outset Zal sat as a guest; in those days two years passed.
sentences.s381-s23: Lohrasp the king was inside Balkh; none of the Iranians or the army remained.
sentences.s381-s24: Except for seven hundred fire‑worshippers — shouting 'Ha!' — they stood before the fire with their hands raised.
sentences.s381-s17: They came to the soothsayer Esfandiyar; the royal princes came all together.
sentences.s381-s18: They kept the father in peace; they left him alone in his prison.
sentences.s381-s19: Then news reached the commander of China that the king, out of suspicion, had fallen into hatred.
sentences.s381-s20: Khosrow was angered with Esfandiyar and sent him, humiliated, to prison and bonds.
sentences.s381-s37: The seeker of the secret took the road to Balkh, where, at an unfortunate hour, the king...
sentences.s381-s38: In the king's inner chamber he saw Gushtasp; he saw a worshipper and Lohrasp.
sentences.s381-s39: So it came to pass; he went before the khaghan and spoke, and he fell to the ground before him.
sentences.s381-s40: When Arjasp became aware, he rejoiced; he was freed from long-standing sorrow.
sentences.s381-s33: He does not drive them onto a path of nothingness nor send them onto a roadless course; from Iran they go away frightened and knowing.
sentences.s381-s34: There was a certain magician; his name was Setūh — a placer (or frustrator) of the road and a secret-seeker.
sentences.s381-s35: I said softly: 'O name-seeker, what should I tell you? Whatever I should, I will tell.'
sentences.s381-s36: The Chinese king said: 'Stride into Iran; see the guardian of the fire to learn which...'
